source: Linehan, M.M. (2015). DBT Skills Training Manual. 2nd Edition.
description: Evidence-based skills from Dialectical Behavior Therapy, designed for emotion regulation, distress tolerance, interpersonal effectiveness, and mindfulness.

strategies.name: DEAR MAN (Interpersonal Effectiveness)
strategies.description: DBT skill for asking for what you want or saying no effectively while maintaining relationships and self-respect.
steps: D - DESCRIBE the situation objectively, stick to facts
E - EXPRESS your feelings and opinions about the situation
A - ASSERT yourself by asking clearly for what you want or saying no
R - REINFORCE by explaining positive effects of getting what you want
M - stay MINDFUL by focusing on your goal, ignore attacks
A - APPEAR confident through posture, tone, eye contact
N - NEGOTIATE by being willing to give to get, offer alternatives
strategies.time_required: 10-20 minutes to prepare, varies for conversation
strategies.contraindications: Not appropriate during crisis; practice first in lower-stakes situations

strategies.name: GIVE (Relationship Maintenance)
strategies.description: DBT skill for maintaining positive relationships while handling difficult interactions.
steps: G - be GENTLE: no attacks, threats, or judgments; be courteous and temperate
I - act INTERESTED: listen and be interested in the other person
V - VALIDATE: acknowledge the other person's feelings and difficulties
E - use an EASY manner: smile, use humor, be light-hearted when appropriate
strategies.contraindications: Not for abusive situations; maintain safety first

strategies.name: FAST (Self-Respect Effectiveness)
strategies.description: DBT skill for maintaining self-respect in interpersonal situations.
steps: F - be FAIR to yourself and to the other person
A - no APOLOGIES for being alive, having an opinion, or making a request
S - STICK to your values; don't compromise values for mood or approval
T - be TRUTHFUL; don't lie, exaggerate, or act helpless when you're not

strategies.name: STOP Skill (Crisis Intervention)
strategies.description: Immediate skill to prevent impulsive action when emotionally overwhelmed.
steps: S - STOP: Freeze! Don't move a muscle. Stay in control
T - Take a step back: Take a breath. Don't let feelings make you act impulsively
O - OBSERVE: Notice what is going on inside and outside you
P - PROCEED mindfully: Act with awareness. Consider what will make the situation better or worse

strategies.name: Opposite Action
strategies.description: Do the opposite of your emotion's action urge when the emotion doesn't fit the facts or acting on it would be harmful.
steps: Identify the emotion and its action urge
Check if emotion fits the facts of the situation
Check if acting on the urge would be effective
If emotion doesn't fit facts or action won't help: Do the OPPOSITE
For fear: Approach what you're afraid of (when safe)
For anger: Gently avoid, be kind, empathize
For shame: Share with safe others, approach don't hide
For sadness: Get active, engage (opposite of withdrawal)
Do opposite action ALL THE WAY - with body language, facial expression, voice
strategies.contraindications: Don't use when emotion DOES fit the facts (e.g., real danger); requires skill to assess situation accurately

strategies.name: Check the Facts
strategies.description: Reduce emotional intensity by checking whether your interpretations and assumptions fit the actual facts.
steps: Ask: What is the emotion I want to change?
Ask: What is the prompting event for my emotion?
Ask: What are my interpretations, thoughts, and assumptions about the event?
Ask: Am I assuming a threat? What is the catastrophe?
Ask: Does my emotion and its intensity fit the actual facts?
List FACTS that support emotion
List FACTS that don't support emotion
Consider alternative interpretations
Practice looking at all sides of the situation
If emotion doesn't fit facts, use Opposite Action

strategies.name: IMPROVE the Moment
strategies.description: Seven ways to improve a distressing moment using distraction and self-soothing.
steps: I - IMAGERY: Imagine relaxing scenes, a safe place, or things going well
M - MEANING: Find or create meaning in the suffering (Viktor Frankl approach)
P - PRAYER: Open your heart to a greater wisdom (or universe, higher power)
R - RELAXATION: Relax muscles, breathe deeply, use half-smile
O - ONE thing in the moment: Focus attention on just what you're doing now
V - VACATION: Take a break from it all for a brief time
E - ENCOURAGEMENT: Cheer-lead yourself - 'I can stand this', 'It will pass'

strategies.name: Self-Soothe with Five Senses
strategies.description: Comfort and nurture yourself through your five senses during distress.
steps: VISION: Look at beautiful things - nature, art, candle flame, sunset
HEARING: Listen to soothing music, sounds of nature, singing birds
SMELL: Use pleasant scents - perfume, flowers, coffee, fresh air
TASTE: Enjoy flavors mindfully - tea, chocolate, a favorite food
TOUCH: Feel comforting textures - soft blanket, warm bath, pet's fur, massage
Be intentional and mindful with each sense
Notice what works best for you
strategies.contraindications: Avoid if it leads to harmful behaviors (e.g., overeating, substance use)

strategies.name: Wise Mind
strategies.description: Access the integration of emotional mind and reasonable mind - your inner wisdom.
steps: Find a quiet place and get comfortable
Close your eyes and focus on your breath
Ask: What does my Emotion Mind say? (feelings, urges)
Ask: What does my Reasonable Mind say? (logic, facts)
Breathe and drop into your center
Ask your Wise Mind: 'What is the wise thing to do here?'
Listen without forcing an answer
Trust the knowing that arises from your core
Notice: Wise Mind feels right in the gut, has a quality of 'just knowing'
Take action from this place of integrated wisdom
strategies.contraindications: May be difficult initially; becomes easier with practice

strategies.name: Accumulate Positive Emotions (Short-term)
strategies.description: Build positive experiences in the moment to reduce vulnerability to negative emotions.
steps: Make a list of pleasant activities that are possible now
Examples: listen to music, take a walk, call a friend, watch comedy, read, cook
Choose one activity you can do right now
Do the activity MINDFULLY - be fully present
Let go of multitasking
Focus on positive sensations and emotions as they arise
Repeat daily to build emotional resilience
Notice what activities work best for your mood

meta.evidence_base: All strategies are from evidence-based DBT protocol, supported by multiple RCTs showing effectiveness for emotion regulation, interpersonal effectiveness, and distress tolerance.
meta.target_populations: Originally developed for Borderline Personality Disorder, now widely used for depression, anxiety, PTSD, eating disorders, and general emotion dysregulation.
meta.training_note: While these strategies can be used independently, formal DBT training provides optimal skill development and includes individual therapy, group skills training, phone coaching, and therapist consultation team.
